IGCSE Physics 0625 · Year 10

End-of-Year Physics Revision Support 2026

← Dr Pop's Enriched Revision

📋 Syllabus Checklist — Topics 1, 2, 3, 5 & 6

Clean checklist first. Full syllabus wording is hidden underneath each row.

Grey rows are part of IGCSE Physics, but not in this Year 10 End-of-year exam.
0
Ticked
–
In exam
💡 How to use this: Tick the visible rows for revision progress. Open the details only when you want the exact Cambridge syllabus wording or need to check what is excluded from this school exam.
⚠️ Scope reminder: Topic 3 refraction, lenses, dispersion and EM spectrum are not assessed in this Year 10 End-of-year exam. Topic 6 only covers Solar System and life cycle of stars; 6.2.3 Universe is not assessed in this exam.
1Motion, Forces and Energy
1.1Physical quantities & measurement
1.1
Use measuring instruments; calculate averages from repeated/multiple measurements; recognise scalars and vectors; find resultant vectors at right angles.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Describe the use of rulers and measuring cylinders to find length or volume.
  • Describe how to measure time intervals using clocks and digital timers.
  • Determine an average value for a small distance or short time by measuring multiples, including the period of a pendulum.

Supplement:

  • Understand scalar and vector quantities.
  • Know scalar examples: distance, speed, time, mass, energy and temperature.
  • Know vector examples: force, weight, velocity, acceleration, momentum, electric field strength and gravitational field strength.
  • Determine, by calculation or graphically, the resultant of two vectors at right angles, limited to forces or velocities.
Supp
✓
1.2Motion
1.2
Speed, velocity, acceleration, distance-time graphs, speed-time graphs, gradient and area methods, free fall and terminal velocity.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Define speed as distance travelled per unit time; recall and use v = s/t.
  • Define velocity as speed in a given direction.
  • Recall and use average speed = total distance travelled / total time taken.
  • Sketch, plot and interpret distance-time and speed-time graphs.
  • Identify rest, constant speed, acceleration and deceleration from data or graph shape.
  • Calculate speed from the gradient of a straight-line section of a distance-time graph.
  • Calculate distance from the area under a speed-time graph for constant speed or constant acceleration.
  • State that g near Earth is approximately constant and approximately 9.8 m/s².

Supplement:

  • Define acceleration as change in velocity per unit time; recall and use a = Δv/Δt.
  • Identify constant and changing acceleration from data or graph shape.
  • Calculate acceleration from the gradient of a speed-time graph.
  • Know deceleration is negative acceleration and use this in calculations.
  • Describe falling in a uniform gravitational field with and without air/liquid resistance, including terminal velocity.
Supp
✓
1.3–1.4Mass, weight and density
1.3–1.4
Mass, weight, gravitational field strength, balances, density, displacement method and floating/sinking decisions.
Full specification detail

Mass and weight:

  • State that mass is a measure of the quantity of matter in an object at rest relative to the observer.
  • State that weight is a gravitational force on an object that has mass.
  • Define gravitational field strength as force per unit mass; recall and use g = W/m and know it is equivalent to acceleration of free fall.
  • Know that weights and masses may be compared using a balance.
  • Supplement: Describe and use weight as the effect of a gravitational field on a mass.

Density:

  • Define density as mass per unit volume; recall and use ρ = m/V.
  • Describe how to determine density of a liquid, a regular solid and an irregular solid which sinks, including displacement method.
  • Determine whether an object floats using density data.
  • Supplement: Determine whether one liquid will float on another using density data, where the liquids do not mix.
Supp
✓
1.5Forces
1.5.1
Effects of forces: deformation, load-extension graphs, resultant forces, friction, Newton’s first law, spring constant, F = ma and circular motion.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Know forces may produce changes in size and shape.
  • Sketch, plot and interpret load-extension graphs for an elastic solid and describe the experiment.
  • Determine the resultant of two or more forces in one straight line.
  • Know an object remains at rest or continues in a straight line at constant speed unless acted on by a resultant force.
  • State that resultant force may change velocity by changing direction or speed.
  • Describe solid friction and know drag acts in liquids and gases.

Supplement:

  • Define spring constant as force per unit extension; recall and use k = F/x.
  • Define and use limit of proportionality for a load-extension graph.
  • Recall and use F = ma and know force and acceleration are in the same direction.
  • Describe circular motion qualitatively: greater force can increase speed, reduce radius, or support greater mass depending on what is kept constant.
Supp
✓
1.5.2
Moments, principle of moments, balancing a beam and equilibrium.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Describe the moment of a force as a measure of turning effect and give everyday examples.
  • Define moment = force × perpendicular distance from the pivot; recall and use the equation.
  • Apply the principle of moments to situations with one force each side of the pivot, including balancing a beam.
  • State that, when there is no resultant force and no resultant moment, an object is in equilibrium.

Supplement:

  • Apply the principle of moments to situations with more than one force each side of the pivot.
  • Describe an experiment to demonstrate there is no resultant moment on an object in equilibrium.
Supp
✓
1.5.3
Centre of gravity and stability of simple objects.
Full specification detail
  • State what is meant by centre of gravity.
  • Describe an experiment to determine the centre of gravity of an irregularly shaped plane lamina.
  • Describe qualitatively how the position of the centre of gravity affects stability.
Core
✓
1.6
Momentum, impulse, conservation of momentum and resultant force as rate of change of momentum.
Full specification detail
  • Define momentum as mass × velocity; recall and use p = mv.
  • Define impulse as force × time for which force acts; recall and use impulse = FΔt = Δ(mv).
  • Apply conservation of momentum to simple problems in one dimension.
  • Define resultant force as change in momentum per unit time; recall and use F = Δp/Δt.
Supp
✓
1.7–1.8Energy, work, power and pressure
1.7
Energy stores and transfers, conservation, work done, energy resources, efficiency and power.
Full specification detail

Energy:

  • State energy stores: kinetic, gravitational potential, chemical, elastic/strain, nuclear, electrostatic and internal/thermal.
  • Describe transfers between stores by mechanical work, electrical work, heating, and electromagnetic, sound and other waves.
  • Apply conservation of energy to simple examples and flow diagrams.
  • Supplement: use Ek = ½mv² and ΔEp = mgΔh; apply conservation to complex examples including Sankey diagrams.

Work, resources and power:

  • Understand mechanical or electrical work done equals energy transferred; use W = Fd = ΔE.
  • Describe ways of generating useful energy/electrical power from fossil fuels, biofuels, water, geothermal, nuclear fuel and solar resources.
  • Describe advantages and disadvantages in terms of renewability, availability, reliability, scale and environmental impact.
  • Define and use efficiency in terms of energy or power.
  • Define power as work done per unit time and energy transferred per unit time; use P = W/t and P = ΔE/t.
Supp
✓
1.8
Pressure in solids and liquids, including force/area and liquid pressure with depth and density.
Full specification detail
  • Define pressure as force per unit area; recall and use p = F/A.
  • Describe how pressure varies with force and area in everyday examples.
  • Describe qualitatively how pressure beneath the surface of a liquid changes with depth and density.
  • Supplement: recall and use Δp = ρgΔh for change in pressure beneath a liquid.
Supp
✓
2Thermal Physics
2.1
States of matter, particle model, gas pressure, Brownian motion, absolute zero, Kelvin temperature and pV = constant.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Know distinguishing properties of solids, liquids and gases and the terms for changes of state, except gas to solid and solid to gas.
  • Describe particle structure in terms of arrangement, separation and motion, using simple diagrams.
  • Describe the relationship between particle motion and temperature, including absolute zero where particles have least kinetic energy.
  • Describe gas pressure and pressure changes in terms of particle motion and collisions with a surface.
  • Know random motion of microscopic particles in suspension is evidence for the kinetic particle model and explain Brownian motion.
  • Describe qualitatively the effect on pressure of fixed mass of gas when temperature changes at constant volume or volume changes at constant temperature.
  • Convert between kelvin and Celsius using T(K) = θ(°C) + 273.

Supplement:

  • Know forces, distances and motion of particles affect properties of solids, liquids and gases.
  • Describe gas pressure in terms of forces from particle collisions creating force per unit area.
  • Use atoms/molecules distinctly from microscopic particles in Brownian motion.
  • Recall and use pV = constant for a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature, including graphs.
Supp
✓
2.2
Thermal expansion, internal energy, specific heat capacity, melting, boiling, evaporation and cooling.
Full specification detail

Thermal expansion and temperature:

  • Describe thermal expansion of solids, liquids and gases at constant pressure.
  • Describe everyday applications and consequences of expansion.
  • Supplement: explain relative expansion of solids, liquids and gases using motion and arrangement of particles.
  • Know that a rise in temperature increases internal energy.
  • Supplement: describe temperature increase as increased average kinetic energies of all particles.
  • Define specific heat capacity as energy required per unit mass per unit temperature increase; use c = ΔE/(mΔθ).
  • Describe experiments to measure specific heat capacity of a solid and a liquid.

Melting, boiling and evaporation:

  • Describe melting and boiling as energy input without temperature change.
  • Know water melts at 0°C and boils at 100°C at standard atmospheric pressure.
  • Describe condensation and solidification in terms of particles.
  • Describe evaporation as escape of more energetic particles from the surface and know it causes cooling.
  • Supplement: describe boiling vs evaporation, factors affecting evaporation, and cooling of an object in contact with evaporating liquid.
Supp
✓
2.3
Conduction, convection, radiation, surface colour/texture and everyday consequences of thermal energy transfer.
Full specification detail

Conduction:

  • Describe experiments to show good and bad thermal conductors.
  • Supplement: describe conduction in solids by lattice vibrations and in metals also by free/delocalised electrons.
  • Describe why conduction is bad in gases and most liquids.

Convection and radiation:

  • Know convection is important in liquids and gases.
  • Explain convection in terms of density changes and describe experiments.
  • Know thermal radiation is infrared radiation and all objects emit it.
  • Know thermal radiation does not require a medium.
  • Describe how colour and texture affect emission, absorption and reflection of infrared.
  • Supplement: constant temperature requires energy away at same rate as energy received; describe what happens if these rates differ; know how Earth’s temperature is affected by radiation balance.
  • Describe experiments for good/bad emitters and absorbers; know emission rate depends on surface temperature and area.
  • Explain everyday applications including pans, room heating, fire and car radiators.
Supp
✓
3Waves
⛔ Not in this Year 10 End-of-year exam: 3.2.2 Refraction · 3.2.3 Thin lenses · 3.2.4 Dispersion · 3.3 Electromagnetic spectrum.
3.1
General wave properties: energy transfer, wave terms, v = fλ, transverse/longitudinal waves, reflection, refraction and diffraction in ripple tanks.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Know waves transfer energy without transferring matter.
  • Describe wave motion using ropes, springs and water waves.
  • Describe wavefront, wavelength, frequency, crest, trough, amplitude and wave speed.
  • Recall and use v = fλ.
  • Know transverse vibration is at right angles to propagation; EM radiation, water waves and seismic S-waves can be modelled as transverse.
  • Know longitudinal vibration is parallel to propagation; sound and seismic P-waves can be modelled as longitudinal.
  • Describe reflection, refraction due to speed change, and diffraction through a narrow gap.
  • Describe ripple tank demonstrations of reflection, refraction and diffraction at gaps/edges.

Supplement:

  • Describe how wavelength and gap size affect diffraction through a gap.
  • Describe how wavelength affects diffraction at an edge.
Supp
✓
3.2.1
Reflection of light: normal, angles of incidence/reflection, i = r and plane mirror images.
Full specification detail
  • Define and use normal, angle of incidence and angle of reflection.
  • Describe a plane mirror image: same size, same distance from mirror, virtual.
  • State and use angle of incidence = angle of reflection.
  • Supplement: use simple constructions, measurements and calculations for reflection by plane mirrors.
Supp
✓
3.2.2
Refraction, critical angle, total internal reflection, refractive index and optical fibres.
Full specification detail
  • Define and use normal, angle of incidence and angle of refraction.
  • Describe an experiment to show refraction by transparent blocks.
  • Describe light passing through transparent material at boundaries between two media.
  • State the meaning of critical angle.
  • Describe internal reflection and total internal reflection using experimental and everyday examples.
  • Supplement: define refractive index as ratio of speeds in two regions; use n = sin i / sin r and n = 1 / sin c; describe optical fibres, especially in telecommunications.
Not tested
–
3.2.3
Converging/diverging lenses, ray diagrams, image descriptions and vision correction.
Full specification detail
  • Describe the action of thin converging and diverging lenses on a parallel beam of light.
  • Define focal length, principal axis and principal focus.
  • Draw and use ray diagrams for real images from converging lenses.
  • Describe images using enlarged/same size/diminished, upright/inverted and real/virtual.
  • Know a virtual image is formed when diverging rays are extrapolated backwards and cannot be projected on a screen.
  • Supplement: draw virtual image ray diagrams for converging lenses; describe magnifying glass; describe lenses for long- and short-sightedness.
Not tested
–
3.2.4
Dispersion of white light, visible spectrum order and monochromatic light.
Full specification detail
  • Describe dispersion of white light by a glass prism.
  • Know the traditional seven colours of the visible spectrum in order of frequency and wavelength.
  • Supplement: recall that visible light of a single frequency is monochromatic.
Not tested
–
3.3
Electromagnetic spectrum, uses, harmful effects, satellite communication and digital/analogue signalling.
Full specification detail
  • Know EM spectrum regions in order of frequency and wavelength.
  • Know all EM waves travel at the same high speed in a vacuum.
  • Describe typical uses of radio, microwaves, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-rays and gamma rays.
  • Describe harmful effects of excessive EM exposure.
  • Know satellite communication is mainly by microwaves.
  • Supplement: know c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum/air; describe communication systems using microwaves, radio waves and optical fibres; know digital vs analogue signals; explain benefits of digital signalling.
Not tested
–
3.4
Sound: vibrations, longitudinal waves, audible range, speed in air, amplitude, frequency, echo and ultrasound.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Describe production of sound by vibrating sources.
  • Describe the longitudinal nature of sound waves.
  • State audible range as 20 Hz to 20 000 Hz.
  • Know a medium is needed for sound.
  • Know speed of sound in air is approximately 330–350 m/s.
  • Describe a method to determine speed of sound in air using distance and time.
  • Describe how amplitude and frequency affect loudness and pitch.
  • Describe echo as reflection of sound.
  • Define ultrasound as sound above 20 kHz.

Supplement:

  • Describe compression and rarefaction.
  • Know sound generally travels faster in solids than liquids, and faster in liquids than gases.
  • Describe ultrasound uses in non-destructive testing, soft tissue scanning and sonar, including depth/distance calculations.
Supp
✓
5Nuclear Physics
5.1
Atomic and nuclear structure, ions, proton/nucleon number, isotopes, nuclide notation, fission and fusion.
Full specification detail

Atom and nucleus:

  • Describe an atom as a positively charged nucleus with negatively charged electrons in orbit.
  • Know atoms can form positive ions by losing electrons or negative ions by gaining electrons.
  • Supplement: describe how alpha scattering supports the nuclear model: small nucleus, mostly empty space, most mass in nucleus, positively charged nucleus.
  • Describe nucleus composition in terms of protons and neutrons.
  • State relative charges of protons, neutrons and electrons as +1, 0 and −1.
  • Define proton number Z and nucleon number A; calculate number of neutrons.
  • Use nuclide notation A/Z X.
  • Explain isotope: same element/proton number, different nucleon number/neutron number.

Supplement:

  • Describe nuclear fission and fusion as splitting/joining nuclei, including nuclide equations and qualitative mass-energy changes.
  • Know relationships between proton number and nuclear charge, and nucleon number and relative nuclear mass.
Supp
✓
5.2.1
Background radiation, sources, detectors, count rate and corrected count rate.
Full specification detail
  • Know what is meant by background radiation.
  • Know significant sources: radon gas, rocks/buildings, food/drink and cosmic rays.
  • Know ionising nuclear radiation can be measured with a detector connected to a counter.
  • Use count rate measured in counts/s or counts/minute.
  • Supplement: use background radiation measurements to determine corrected count rate.
Supp
✓
5.2.2
Alpha, beta and gamma emissions: nature, ionising effect and penetration.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Describe emission of radiation from a nucleus as spontaneous and random in direction.
  • Identify α, β and γ emissions by their nature, relative ionising effects and relative penetrating abilities. β⁺ is not included; β-particles refer to β⁻.
Core
✓
5.2.2+
Deflection of α, β, γ and explaining ionising effects by kinetic energy and charge.
Full specification detail
  • Supplement: describe deflection of α-particles, β-particles and γ-radiation in electric and magnetic fields.
  • Explain relative ionising effects with reference to kinetic energy and electric charge.
Not tested
–
5.2.3
Radioactive decay, change of element, decay equations and stability.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Know radioactive decay is a change in an unstable nucleus that can emit α, β and/or γ radiation, and that these changes are spontaneous and random.
  • State that during α-decay or β-decay, the nucleus changes to that of a different element.

Supplement:

  • Know isotopes may be radioactive due to excess neutrons and/or the nucleus being too heavy.
  • Describe the effect of α-decay, β-decay and γ-emissions on the nucleus, including increased stability and reduced excess neutrons.
  • Know neutron → proton + electron during β-emission.
  • Use decay equations with nuclide notation for α, β and γ emission.
Supp
✓
5.2.4–5.2.5
Half-life, applications of radioisotopes and safety precautions.
Full specification detail

Half-life:

  • Define half-life as the time taken for half the nuclei of a particular isotope in any sample to decay.
  • Use this definition in simple calculations, possibly with tables or decay curves; Core calculations do not include background radiation.
  • Supplement: calculate half-life from data or decay curves where background radiation has not been subtracted.
  • Explain how radiation type and half-life determine isotope choice for smoke alarms, food irradiation, sterilisation, thickness control, and cancer diagnosis/treatment.

Safety:

  • State effects of ionising nuclear radiation: cell death, mutations and cancer.
  • Describe safe moving, use and storage of radioactive materials.
  • Supplement: explain precautions using reduced exposure time, increased distance and shielding.
Supp
✓
6Space Physics
⛔ Exam scope: Only Solar System (6.1) and Life Cycle of Stars (6.2.1–6.2.2) are assessed. Section 6.2.3 Universe is part of IGCSE Physics but not in this Year 10 End-of-year exam.
6.1.1
Earth’s rotation, orbit, seasons, Moon phases and average orbital speed.
Full specification detail
  • Know Earth is a planet that rotates on a tilted axis once in approximately 24 hours; use this to explain apparent daily motion of the Sun and day/night.
  • Know Earth orbits the Sun once in approximately 365 days; use this to explain seasons.
  • Know the Moon orbits Earth in approximately one month; use this to explain the Moon’s phases.
  • Supplement: define average orbital speed using v = 2πr/T, where r is average orbit radius and T is orbital period; recall and use this equation.
Supp
✓
6.1.2
Solar System contents, rocky/gaseous planets, accretion model, gravity, light travel time, elliptical orbits and planetary data.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Describe the Solar System as containing one star, the Sun; eight named planets in order; minor planets including dwarf planets and asteroids; moons; comets and natural satellites.
  • Know the four planets nearest the Sun are rocky and small, and the four furthest are gaseous and large; explain using an accretion model including gravity, elements in interstellar clouds, cloud rotation and accretion disc formation.
  • Know gravitational field strength at a planet surface depends on planet mass and decreases with distance from the planet.
  • Calculate light travel time over significant distances in the Solar System.
  • Know the Sun contains most mass of the Solar System, explaining why planets orbit the Sun.
  • Know gravity from the Sun keeps objects in orbit around it.

Supplement:

  • Know planets, minor planets and comets have elliptical orbits and the Sun is not at the centre except for approximately circular orbits.
  • Analyse planetary data: orbital distance, orbital duration, density, surface temperature and surface gravitational field strength.
  • Know the Sun’s gravitational field strength and planetary orbital speeds decrease with distance from the Sun.
  • Know an object in an elliptical orbit travels faster when closer to the Sun and explain this using conservation of energy.
Supp
✓
6.2.1
Sun as a medium-sized star; mostly hydrogen and helium; radiation regions; fusion in stable stars.
Full specification detail
  • Know the Sun is a star of medium size, consisting mostly of hydrogen and helium.
  • Know the Sun radiates most of its energy in the infrared, visible light and ultraviolet regions of the electromagnetic spectrum.
  • Supplement: know stars are powered by nuclear reactions that release energy and stable stars involve fusion of hydrogen into helium.
Supp
✓
6.2.2
Galaxies, Milky Way, light-years and the life cycle of stars.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • State that galaxies are made of many billions of stars.
  • State that the Sun is a star in the Milky Way.
  • State that other Milky Way stars are much further from Earth than the Sun is.
  • Know astronomical distances can be measured in light-years: the distance travelled by light in one year in space.

Supplement:

  • Know 1 light-year = 9.5 × 10¹⁵ m.
  • Describe star formation from interstellar clouds of gas and dust containing hydrogen.
  • Describe protostar formation as collapse and temperature increase due to internal gravitational attraction.
  • Know a protostar becomes stable when inward gravitational attraction is balanced by outward force due to high central temperature.
  • Know all stars eventually run out of hydrogen fuel.
  • Know most stars become red giants; more massive stars become red supergiants.
  • Know a red giant from a less massive star forms a planetary nebula with a white dwarf at its centre.
  • Know a red supergiant explodes as a supernova, forming a nebula with hydrogen and new heavier elements, leaving a neutron star or black hole.
  • Know a supernova nebula may form new stars with orbiting planets.
Supp
✓
6.2.3
Universe, redshift, Big Bang, CMBR, Hubble constant and age of the Universe estimate.
Full specification detail

Core:

  • Know the Milky Way is one of many billions of galaxies and is about 100 000 light-years in diameter.
  • Describe redshift as an increase in observed wavelength of EM radiation from receding stars and galaxies.
  • Know distant galaxy light appears redshifted compared with light emitted on Earth.
  • Know redshift is evidence that the Universe is expanding and supports the Big Bang Theory.

Supplement:

  • Know CMBR is microwave radiation of a specific frequency observed at all points in space.
  • Explain CMBR was produced shortly after Universe formation and expanded into the microwave region as the Universe expanded.
  • Know galaxy speed can be found from redshift wavelength change.
  • Know galaxy distance can be determined from supernova brightness.
  • Define Hubble constant H₀ = v/d and use this equation.
  • Know current estimate H₀ = 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁸ per second.
  • Know d/v = 1/H₀ estimates the age of the Universe and supports the idea all matter was once at a single point.
Not tested
–
⚡ Topic 1

Motion, Forces & Energy

20 past-paper MCQs from 2022–2025 · Walkthroughs available per question

🧮 Key Formulae
QuantityFormulaNotes
Speedv = s / tm/s
Accelerationa = Δv / Δtm/s²; negative = deceleration
F = maF = maN; F and a in same direction
Resultant forceF = Δp / ΔtN
WeightW = mgg ≈ 10 N/kg
Densityρ = m / Vkg/m³
Momentump = mvkg m/s
ImpulseFΔt = Δ(mv)N s
Spring constantk = F / xN/m
MomentM = F × d⊥N m
Pressurep = F / APa
Liquid pressureΔp = ρgΔhh = depth
WorkW = FdJ
KEKE = ½mv²J
GPEΔEp = mgΔhJ
PowerP = W/t = ΔE/tW
Efficiencyη = useful output / total input × 100%%
📚 Key Vocabulary
TermCAIE DefinitionExam tip
AccelerationRate of change of velocity / change in velocity per unit timeMust say "per unit time"
VelocitySpeed in a stated directionSpeed + direction — always state direction
DecelerationRate of decrease of velocity; negative acceleration"Negative rate of change of velocity" also accepted
MomentumMass × velocity (p = mv)Vector; conserved in all collisions
ImpulseForce × time for which force acts; or change in momentumEither definition accepted
WeightThe gravitational force on an object that has massForce (N) not mass (kg)
Centre of gravityPoint where all the weight of an object appears to act"Appears to act" not "is located"
PressureForce per unit areaPa or N/m²
Moment of a forceForce × perpendicular distance from the pivot; turning effectMust say perpendicular distance
Limit of proportionalityMaximum force up to which extension is directly proportional to loadBeyond this, Hooke's law fails
Gravitational field strengthForce per unit mass on an object in a gravitational fieldg ≈ 10 N/kg on Earth
EfficiencyUseful energy/power output as a fraction (×100%) of total energy/power inputCan be fraction or percentage
Mode
Click Check after each question, or open Walkthrough at any time.
🧠 MCQ Practice — 20 Questions — Topic 1
1Gravitational field strength and spring extension
On the Earth, a spring stretches by 5.0 cm when a mass of 3.0 kg is suspended from one end.

The gravitational field strength on the Moon is ⅙ of that on the Earth.

Which mass, on the Moon, would stretch the spring by the same extension?
A0.50 kg
B3.0 kg
C5.0 kg
D18 kg
How to think through it
1Same extension means the spring needs the same weight force.
2Weight = mg. On the Moon, g is 6 times smaller.
3So the mass must be 6 times larger, not smaller.
⚠Common trap: Weaker gravity means more mass is needed for the same weight.
2Direction of resultant force for circular motion
A car is travelling around a circular track at a constant speed, as shown.

In which direction is the resultant force on the car?
ADirection A
BDirection B
CDirection C
DDirection D
How to think through it
1Circular motion needs an inward resultant force.
2Find the centre of the circular track.
3Choose the arrow pointing towards the centre.
⚠Common trap: Do not choose the direction of motion; the force is towards the centre.
3Resultant force on a satellite in circular orbit
A satellite orbits the Earth at constant speed in a circular orbit.

Which statement is correct?
AThe resultant force on the satellite is zero.
BThe resultant force on the satellite is towards the Earth.
CThe resultant force on the satellite is away from the Earth.
DThe resultant force on the satellite is in the direction of its motion.
How to think through it
1Constant speed does not mean zero resultant force if direction changes.
2A satellite in circular orbit needs an inward force (towards the centre of the orbit).
3For an Earth satellite, that inward force is gravity.
⚠Common trap: Velocity changes because direction changes.
4Distance from area under a speed–time graph
The speed–time graph shows the motion of an object.
How far does the object travel at constant speed?
A25 m
B50 m
C75 m
D125 m
How to think through it
1Distance on a speed-time graph = area under the graph.
2The constant-speed section is the flat top of the graph, from t = 5 s to t = 15 s.
3For a rectangular area, use base × height: 10 s × 5 m/s = 50 m.
⚠Common trap: Do not use final speed × total time unless the speed is constant for the whole journey.
5Resultant velocity from perpendicular components
An aircraft is moving at 60 m/s in a northerly direction when a cross-wind from the east starts to blow at 13 m/s.

What is the magnitude of the aircraft's resultant velocity?
A47 m/s
B59 m/s
C61 m/s
D73 m/s
How to think through it
1Treat the two velocities as perpendicular components that make up a right-angle triangle.
2Use Pythagoras for the resultant speed.
3To check, the answer should be larger than either component alone.
⚠Common trap: Do not add the speeds directly unless they are in the same direction.
6Methods for comparing mass
Two rectangular blocks consist of different materials.
Four methods are suggested to compare the two masses.

1   Compare the accelerations with which they fall freely.
2   Compare the values of their lengths × breadths × heights.
3   Hang each in turn from the same spring. Compare the extensions.
4   Place one in the right-hand pan of a beam balance and the other in the left-hand pan.

Which methods give a comparison of the two masses?
A1, 2 and 3
B1 and 2 only
C3 and 4 only
D4 only
How to think through it
1Ask which methods compare mass rather than size or falling acceleration.
2A mass hanging from a spring works like a spring balance: the weight causes an extension, provided the spring stays within its elastic limit.
3Volume only helps if density is also known.
⚠Common trap: Objects in the same gravitational field have the same free-fall acceleration.
7Resultant of two perpendicular forces
Forces of 3 N and 4 N act at right angles, as shown.
What is the resultant force?
A1 N along XZ
B5 N along XZ
C5 N along OY
D7 N along OY
How to think through it
1The two forces are perpendicular components.
2Use Pythagoras for the size of the resultant.
3The direction is the diagonal between the two force arrows.
⚠Common trap: Do not simply add 3 N and 4 N.
8Terminal velocity — initial and final acceleration
A light ball is held at rest at the top of a tall cliff. It is released and falls through the air, eventually reaching its terminal velocity.

Which row describes the behaviour of the ball as it descends?
Initial accelerationFinal acceleration
A00
B0g
Cg0
Dgg
How to think through it
1At release, speed is zero, so air resistance is negligible.
2Initially the resultant force is weight downward.
3At terminal velocity, resultant force and acceleration are zero.
⚠Common trap: Terminal velocity means constant velocity, not zero velocity.
9Distance from area under a triangular speed–time graph
The graph represents the motion of a vehicle.
What is the distance travelled by the vehicle in 400 s?
A20 m
B400 m
C4000 m
D8000 m
How to think through it
1Distance = area under the speed-time graph.
2For a triangular area, use ½ × base × height.
3Read base from time and height from speed.
⚠Common trap: The gradient gives acceleration, not distance.
10Vector diagram for resultant force
Which vector diagram correctly shows the force Z as the resultant of forces X and Y?
ADiagram A
BDiagram B
CDiagram C
DDiagram D
How to think through it
1Use tip-to-tail vector addition.
2Keep each vector’s length and direction unchanged.
3The resultant goes from the first tail to the final tip.
⚠Common trap: Check the arrowhead direction on the resultant.
11Density of pressurised air
A sealed container of volume 2000 cm³ contains air at high pressure. The container is placed on a top-pan balance which reads 200.00 g. All the air is removed by a vacuum pump and the balance reading changes to 196.00 g.

What was the density of the pressurised air?
A0.00200 g/cm³
B0.098 g/cm³
C4.00 g/cm³
D10.2 g/cm³
How to think through it
1The loss in balance reading is the mass of air removed.
2Use the container volume as the air volume.
3Density = mass ÷ volume.
⚠Common trap: Use the change in mass, not the final balance reading.
12Conditions for equilibrium of three forces
Each diagram shows three forces on a beam.

In which situation is it possible for the three forces shown to be in equilibrium?
ADiagram A
BDiagram B
CDiagram C
DDiagram D
How to think through it
1Equilibrium means zero resultant force and zero resultant moment.
2First compare upward and downward forces: total up = total down.
3Then check moments. Imagine a pivot below the force on the right, so that the force has no turning effect. A larger force (immediately to the left) × shorter distance can be balanced a smaller force (far left) × longer distance in the opposite direction.
⚠Common trap: Balancing forces alone is not enough; the positions of the forces matter.
13Impulse, time and landing force
A gymnast jumps down from a high piece of apparatus. She gradually bends her knees as she lands.

Which effect does this have?
AShe will have a smaller change of momentum as she lands.
BShe will lose less kinetic energy as she lands.
CShe will exert a smaller impulse on the ground as she lands.
DShe will experience a smaller force from the ground as she lands.
How to think through it
1Bending knees increases the stopping time.
2The change in momentum is still the same.
3Greater time means smaller average force.
⚠Common trap: Impulse depends on change in momentum, not on how comfortable the landing feels.
14Meaning of ρ in the liquid pressure equation
The equation Δp = ρgΔh can be used for a liquid.

What is the meaning of the term ρ?
Apressure due to the liquid
Bdensity of the liquid
Ctotal pressure due to the liquid and the air above the liquid
Ddensity of an object placed in the liquid
How to think through it
1In Δp = ρgΔh, match each symbol to a physical quantity.
2g is gravitational field strength and Δh is depth change.
3ρ is the liquid’s density.
⚠Common trap: Use the units kg/m³ to recognise density.
15Resultant force from change in momentum
The momentum of a car changes from 10 000 kg m/s to 15 000 kg m/s in a time of 8.0 s.

What is the resultant force acting on the car?
A630 N
B3100 N
C40 000 N
D200 000 N
How to think through it
1Use F = Δp ÷ Δt.
2Find change in momentum: final momentum − initial momentum.
3Divide by the time taken.
⚠Common trap: Be careful with direction if momentum changes sign.
16Moments — identifying the incorrect statement
Which statement about the moment of a force is not correct?
AIf an object is balanced about a pivot, the resultant moment on the object must be zero.
BThe moment of a force is a measure of its turning effect.
CThe moment of a force about a point is equal to: force × perpendicular distance from the point.
DThe moment of a force about a point increases when the perpendicular distance of the force from the point decreases.
How to think through it
1This asks for the statement that is not correct.
2Recall: moment = force × perpendicular distance from pivot.
3Test each statement against this definition.
⚠Common trap: The distance must be perpendicular to the line of action of the force.
17Liquid pressure depends only on depth
Diagram 1 shows a conical flask containing water. Diagram 2 shows a beaker with the same base area as the flask and containing water of the same depth d.
Which statement is correct?
AThe pressure at P is equal to the pressure at X.
BThe pressure at P is greater than the pressure at Y.
CThe pressure at P is greater than the pressure at X.
DThe pressure at Y is greater than the pressure at X.
How to think through it
1Liquid pressure depends on density, g and depth.
2The same liquid means density is the same.
3Compare vertical depth below the surface, not container shape.
⚠Common trap: Wider containers do not automatically mean greater pressure.
18Impulse for a ball rebounding off a wall
A ball of mass 0.25 kg hits a wall at a speed of 16 m/s. It then rebounds back along its original path at a speed of 12 m/s.

What is the impulse experienced by the ball during its impact with the wall?
A1.0 N s
B3.0 N s
C4.0 N s
D7.0 N s
How to think through it
1Choose one direction as positive.
2The rebound velocity is in the opposite direction, so give it the opposite sign.
3Use impulse = change in momentum.
⚠Common trap: For a rebound, the momentum change is larger than just mv.
19Density from a pressure–depth graph
The graph shows how the pressure due to a liquid varies with the depth beneath the liquid surface. The gravitational field strength g = 9.8 N/kg.
What is the density of the liquid?
A200 kg/m³
B820 kg/m³
C2000 kg/m³
D8200 kg/m³
How to think through it
1For a pressure-depth graph, gradient = pressure change ÷ depth change.
2From Δp = ρgΔh, gradient = ρg.
3Divide the gradient by g to find ρ.
⚠Common trap: Use SI units: Pa and m.
20Power as rate of energy transfer
The engine of a motor vehicle develops a large power.

Which statement is correct?
AThe driving force acting on the vehicle must be large.
BThe engine must have a very large volume.
CThe engine must transfer large amounts of energy each second.
DThe vehicle must be very fast.
How to think through it
1Power is energy transferred per unit time.
2A larger power means more energy each second.
3Check which option says this without adding extra assumptions.
⚠Common trap: Power does not necessarily mean more total energy unless time is known.
🌡️ Topic 2

Thermal Physics

10 past-paper MCQs from 2022–2025

🧮 Key Formulae
QuantityFormulaNotes
Kelvin conversionT(K) = θ(°C) + 273Always convert to K for gas law calculations
Specific heat capacityΔE = mcΔθ  (c = ΔE / mΔθ)E in J
m in kg
c in J/(kg·K)
Boyle's lawp₁V₁ = p₂V₂For a fixed mass at constant temperature
📚 Key Vocabulary
TermCAIE DefinitionExam tip
Absolute zeroTemperature at which particles have the least/minimum kinetic energy; lowest possible temperature (−273°C / 0 K)Particles do NOT stop — minimum KE, not zero KE
Boiling pointTemperature at which a liquid becomes a gas; or at which liquid and gas exist togetherFixed temperature at a given pressure
Specific heat capacityEnergy required per unit mass per unit temperature increaseUse units carefully: J/(kg °C)
EvaporationEscape of more-energetic particles from the surface of a liquid, at any temperature below boiling pointCauses remaining liquid to cool
ConductionTransfer of thermal energy through a material by particle vibrations (and free electrons in metals)Best in solids; worst in gases
ConvectionTransfer of thermal energy by bulk movement of a fluid from hotter to cooler regions due to density differencesOnly in liquids and gases.
Mechanism: heated particles gain KE → move further apart → become less dense → rise; cooler denser fluid sinks, setting up a convection current.
RadiationTransfer of thermal energy by infrared electromagnetic waves; does not require a mediumOnly method by which thermal energy travels through a vacuum
Mode
Click Check after each question, or open Walkthrough at any time.
🧠 MCQ Practice — 10 Questions — Topic 2
1Absolute zero and particle energy
What is the lowest possible temperature (absolute zero) and what happens to the energy of particles at this temperature?
A−273 °C; particles have least kinetic energy
B−273 °C; particles have zero gravitational potential energy
C0 °C; particles have least kinetic energy
D0 °C; particles have zero gravitational potential energy
How to think through it
1First fix the temperature scale: absolute zero is 0 K, which is about −273 °C.
2The particle-energy statement should refer to kinetic energy, not gravitational potential energy.
3At absolute zero, particles do not stop completely; they have the minimum possible kinetic energy.
⚠Watch for: Do not confuse absolute zero with the melting point of ice. This question is testing the lower end of the Kelvin scale and particle KE.
2Particle behaviour after condensation
Which statement about the particles of a substance after condensation is correct?
AThe particles are closer together than in the gas state.
BThe particles move faster than in the gas state.
CThe particles have more kinetic energy than in the gas state.
DThe particles have no attraction for each other.
How to think through it
1Condensation means gas → liquid.
2Compare particle spacing in a gas and in a liquid.
3The substance loses energy during condensation, so check what happens to average speed/KE.
⚠Watch for: Gas particles are far apart. Liquid particles are closer together; condensation does not make particles gain KE.
3Radiation absorption and emission by surface colour
Two otherwise identical cars, one black and one white, are at the same initial temperature. The cars are left in bright sunshine and their temperatures increase. During the night, their temperatures decrease.

Which row correctly describes the two cars?
During the day: which car reaches a higher temperature?During the night: which car cools more quickly?
Ablack carblack car
Bblack carwhite car
Cwhite carblack car
Dwhite carwhite car
How to think through it
1During the day, focus on absorption: dark surfaces absorb more radiation; white surfaces reflect more.
2At night, focus on emission: dark surfaces are also better emitters of infrared radiation.
3Use the two surface rules to match the day and night columns.
⚠Note that: Black surfaces are better at both absorbing and emitting radiation. White surfaces reflect more radiation.
4Gas compression at constant temperature — p–V graph
A fixed mass of air in a cylinder is compressed by a piston so that the volume decreases at constant temperature.

The diagram shows the apparatus.
Which graph shows the relationship between the pressure and the volume of the gas?
AGraph A
BGraph B
CGraph C
DGraph D
How to think through it
1Fixed mass and constant temperature points to Boyle’s law.
2Use the pattern p×V = constant: smaller volume means larger pressure.
3Look for an inverse curve, not a straight-line relationship.
⚠Watch for: A straight line suggests direct proportionality. Constant-temperature gas compression gives an inverse relationship.
5Melting at constant temperature — internal energy
Which statement describes what happens as ice at 0 °C melts to become water?
AThe internal energy increases and the temperature stays constant.
BThe internal energy stays constant and the temperature increases.
CThe kinetic energy of particles increases and the temperature stays constant.
DThe potential energy of particles decreases and the temperature increases.
How to think through it
1During melting, temperature stays constant, so average kinetic energy does not increase.
2Energy is still being supplied; it changes the arrangement/separation of particles.
3Decide which statement keeps temperature fixed but increases internal energy.
⚠Watch for: If the temperature is constant, average kinetic energy is constant. The supplied energy changes the potential part of internal energy.
6Thermal conduction and free electrons
The table shows the state of materials P, Q, R and S, and whether each one contains free electrons.

Which material is the best conductor of thermal energy?
AP
BQ
CR
DS
How to think through it
1First identify the state of matter. Thermal conduction is usually strongest in solids.
2Then check for free electrons. Metals conduct especially well because they have free electrons.
⚠Watch for: Do not choose from state alone. A solid without free electrons is usually less effective than a metal-like solid.
7Brownian motion and the kinetic model
Extremely small pollen grains in water are viewed through a microscope. The grains are seen to move continually and randomly.

What is the reason for this random movement?
AWater molecules collide with the pollen grains from all sides at random.
BConvection currents form in the water and carry the pollen grains.
CThe pollen grains repel each other electrically.
DWater evaporates from one side of each pollen grain.
How to think through it
1The pollen grains are visible; the water molecules causing the motion are not.
2Random motion needs random impacts from many directions.
3Reject explanations that would produce an organised flow or one-sided effect.
⚠Watch for: Convection would move fluid in a pattern. Brownian motion is irregular because the collisions are random.
8Specific heat capacity calculation — heat transfer between objects
A glass beaker contains a mass of 0.20 kg of water at a temperature of 20 °C.
The specific heat capacity of water is 4200 J/(kg °C).

A brass block of mass 0.15 kg is at a temperature of 170 °C.
The block is carefully lowered into the beaker of water.

The final temperature of both the water and brass is 30 °C.

What is the specific heat capacity of the brass?
(Assume that no water is lost and no thermal energy has been transferred to the beaker or surroundings.)
A400 J/(kg °C)
B660 J/(kg °C)
C1200 J/(kg °C)
D5600 J/(kg °C)
How to think through it
1Assume no energy is lost to the beaker or surroundings.
2For the water, write: ΔE(water) = mcΔθ.
3For the brass, write: ΔE(brass) = mcΔθ.
4The energy gained by the water equals the energy lost by the brass, i.e. ΔE(water) = ΔE(brass).
5For the water, the temperature rises from 20 °C → 30 °C. For the brass, the temperature falls from 170 °C → 30 °C. Substitute the masses and solve for c of brass.
⚠Watch for: The brass temperature change is 170 °C to 30 °C, not 170 °C to 20 °C.
9Cooling sealed gas at constant volume
A flask with a tight-fitting stopper contains a gas at room temperature. The gas and flask are cooled.
Which row describes what happens to the pressure and the number of molecules of gas in the flask?
Pressure of gasNumber of molecules of gas
Adecreasesstays the same
Bdecreasesdecreases
Cstays the samestays the same
Dstays the samedecreases
How to think through it
1The stopper makes this a sealed system: no molecules escape.
2Cooling reduces the particles’ average KE/speed. With fixed volume, the gas pressure falls.
⚠Watch for: Cooling the gas does not remove molecules. In a sealed flask the number stays the same.
10Surface area and rate of cooling by radiation
Two samples of the same material have the same mass but different surface areas. Each sample is heated to the same temperature and then left to cool to room temperature.
Which row describes the cooling of the two samples?
Sample with larger surface areaSample with smaller surface area
Acools more slowlycools more quickly
Bcools more slowlycools more slowly
Ccools more quicklycools more quickly
Dcools more quicklycools more slowly
How to think through it
1Cooling by radiation happens at the surface.
2Same mass, material and starting temperature means surface area is the key difference.
3The larger surface area emits more energy per second, so compare the cooling rates from that.
⚠Watch for: Larger surface area means faster energy loss by radiation, not slower.
🌊 Topic 3

Waves

10 past-paper MCQs from 2022–2025

⛔ Not in your exam: Refraction, thin lenses and dispersion. Everything else is assessed.
🧮 Key Formulae
QuantityFormulaNotes
Wave speedv = fλv in m/s; f in Hz; λ in m
PeriodT = 1 / fT in s
Speed of EM wavesc = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/sIn vacuum
Law of reflectioni = rBoth angles are measured from the normal
Ultrasound depthd = v × t / 2÷2 because pulse travels there AND back
Extra refraction formulae — useful, but not in your Year 10 End-of-year exam
These are useful for IGCSE refraction, but are not assessed in our Year 10 End-of-year exam.
QuantityFormulaNotes
Refractive indexn = sin i / sin ri = angle of incidence; r = angle of refraction
Speed in mediumn = c / vv = speed in the medium
📚 Key Vocabulary
TermCAIE DefinitionExam tip
FrequencyNumber of complete cycles/vibrations passing a point per unit time (per second)Unit: Hz = cycles per second
Longitudinal waveA wave in which particle vibrations are parallel to the direction of wave propagationExamples: sound, seismic P-waves
Compression & RarefactionCompression: particles closer together / higher pressure. Rarefaction: further apart / lower pressure.Unique to longitudinal waves
UltrasoundSound with a frequency higher than 20 kHzf > 20 000 Hz
Critical angleThe angle of incidence in the denser medium at which the angle of refraction is exactly 90°At this angle: refraction at 90° and strong reflection occur.
Above this angle: total internal reflection occurs.
Total internal reflectionAll light is reflected with no refraction when light in a denser medium hits a boundary with a less dense medium at an angle greater than the critical angle.Both conditions must be met: denser to less dense, and angle greater than the critical angle.
MonochromaticLight/electromagnetic radiation of a single frequencyLaser light is monochromatic; white light is not
📡 EM Spectrum at a Glance
WaveRelative λKey usesHazards
RadioLongestTV, radio, astronomy, RFIDMinimal
MicrowaveLongSatellite TV, mobile phones, ovensInternal heating of body cells
InfraredMedium-longRemote controls, thermal imaging, optical fibresSkin burns
VisibleMediumVision, photography, illuminationEye damage if intense
UltravioletMedium-shortSecurity marking, sterilising water, fake note detectionSkin cancer, eye damage
X-raysShortMedical scanning, security scannersCell damage, cancer
Gamma (γ)ShortestSterilising food/equipment, cancer treatmentMutation, cell damage
Mode
Click Check after each question, or open Walkthrough at any time.
🧠 MCQ Practice — 10 Questions — Topic 3
1Amplitude and wavelength from a wave diagram
The diagram shows a wave.
What are the amplitude and the wavelength of the wave?
AAmplitude = 3 cm, wavelength = 4 cm
BAmplitude = 3 cm, wavelength = 8 cm
CAmplitude = 6 cm, wavelength = 4 cm
DAmplitude = 6 cm, wavelength = 8 cm
How to think through it
1Find the rest position first — the dotted line that cuts through the middle of the wave.
2Amplitude is from the rest position to a crest or trough. Do not use the full crest-to-trough height.
3Wavelength is one full wave: crest to next crest, or trough to next trough.
⚠Common trap: Crest-to-trough height is twice the amplitude. Always measure amplitude from the centre line.
2Diffraction — changes that reduce wave curvature
Parallel waves in a ripple tank are diffracted as they pass through a gap in a barrier.
Three changes are listed:

1   Decrease the wavelength of the waves.
2   Increase the amplitude of the waves.
3   Decrease the size of the gap.

Which changes will cause the shape of the diffracted waves to be less curved?
A1, 2 and 3
B1 and 3 only
C1 only
D2 and 3 only
How to think through it
1Less curved means less diffraction.
2Diffraction is reduced when the wavelength is smaller compared with the gap.
3Amplitude changes loudness (sound) / height (transverse waves), not the amount of diffraction.
⚠Common trap: A smaller gap gives more spreading, not less.
3Wave speed, frequency and wavelength calculation
An earthquake-monitoring station records the arrival of 16 complete wavelengths of an earthquake wave in a time of 20 s. The speed of the earthquake wave is 6.0 km/s.

What is the wavelength of the earthquake wave?
A1.3 × 10⁻⁴ m
B2.1 × 10⁻⁴ m
C4.8 × 10³ m
D7.5 × 10³ m
How to think through it
1Frequency means waves per second: 16 waves in 20 s, so calculate 16 ÷ 20 first.
2Use v = fλ, so λ = v ÷ f.
3Keep the speed in km/s, so the wavelength comes out in km.
⚠Common trap: Do not mix m and km unless you convert everything carefully.
4Wavefronts and wave properties
A vertical stick is dipped up and down in water at P. In two seconds, three wave crests spread out on the surface.
Which statement about the waves is correct?
ADistance X is the amplitude of the waves.
BDistance Y is the wavelength of the waves.
CEach circle represents a wavefront.
DThe frequency of the waves is 3 Hz.
How to think through it
1A wavefront is a crest line viewed from the top. From a point source, the wavefronts are circles.
2Frequency is waves per second. Three crests in two seconds is not 3 Hz.
3Check X and Y against the definitions of amplitude and wavelength.
⚠Common trap: Crest-to-crest distance is wavelength. Amplitude is a height, not a horizontal distance.
5Sound crossing a boundary
A sound wave travels from air into water.

Which row describes what happens to the frequency and the wavelength of the wave?
FrequencyWavelength
Adecreasesincreases
Bdecreasesstays the same
Cstays the samedecreases
Dstays the sameincreases
How to think through it
1The source controls the frequency. Changing medium does not change frequency.
2Sound travels faster in water than in air.
3Use v = fλ: if v increases and f stays the same, decide what happens to λ.
⚠Common trap: Frequency stays the same at a boundary. Speed and wavelength can change.
6Sound wave compressions and rarefactions
The diagram shows the air molecules in part of a sound wave at a particular moment in time.
Which statement is not correct?
AEarlier, there was compression at X.
BLater, there will be a rarefaction at X.
CThis part of the wave is travelling horizontally across the page.
DThis part of the wave is travelling towards the top of the page.
How to think through it
1Compressions are the crowded bands. Rarefactions are the spread-out regions.
2The pattern repeats across the page, so the wave travels horizontally.
3The particles vibrate back and forth parallel to the wave direction.
⚠Common trap: Do not confuse particle vibration with the wave pattern. Sound is longitudinal.
7Amplitude and frequency — loudness and pitch of a siren
A police car with its siren sounding is stationary in heavy traffic. A pedestrian notices that, although the loudness of the sound produced does not change, the pitch varies.

Which row describes the amplitude and the frequency of the sound?
AmplitudeFrequency
Aconstantconstant
Bconstantvarying
Cvaryingconstant
Dvaryingvarying
How to think through it
1Loudness depends on amplitude.
2Pitch depends on frequency.
3Translate the words: loudness unchanged, pitch varies.
⚠Common trap: Amplitude controls loudness; frequency controls pitch.
8Wave speed from frequency and wavelength
A passing boat causes a floating object on a lake to bob up and down 18 times in 12 s. The wavelength of the wave created by the boat is 48 cm.

What is the velocity of these water waves?
A32 cm/s
B72 cm/s
C576 cm/s
D864 cm/s
How to think through it
1Frequency is bobs per second: 18 bobs in 12 s.
2Use v = fλ.
3λ is given in cm, so the speed will be in cm/s.
⚠Common trap: 18 is the number of bobs, not the frequency. Divide by the time first.
9Frequency range of visible light
Visible light has wavelengths in the range 4.0 × 10⁻⁷ m to 7.0 × 10⁻⁷ m.

What is the range of the frequencies of visible light?
A0.12 Hz to 0.21 Hz
B120 Hz to 210 Hz
C4.3 × 10¹¹ Hz to 7.5 × 10¹¹ Hz
D4.3 × 10¹⁴ Hz to 7.5 × 10¹⁴ Hz
How to think through it
1Use f = c ÷ λ, with c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s.
2Shorter wavelength gives higher frequency.
3Check the powers of ten. Visible light frequencies are around 10¹⁴ Hz.
⚠Common trap: A tiny error in standard form can move the answer by a factor of 1000.
10Time delay between seeing smoke and hearing sound
Student X fires a starting pistol which produces smoke and sound. Student Y is standing 100 m away and sees the smoke the instant it is produced. The speed of sound in air is 340 m/s.

What is the time delay between Student Y seeing the smoke and hearing the sound?
A0.29 s
B0.59 s
C1.7 s
D3.4 s
How to think through it
1The smoke is seen almost instantly because light is much faster than sound.
2The delay is the time for sound to travel 100 m.
3Use time = distance ÷ speed. There is no return journey.
⚠Common trap: Do not double the distance. The sound travels one way from X to Y.
☢️ Topic 5

Nuclear Physics

10 past-paper MCQs from 2022–2025

⚛️ Radiation Properties
PropertyAlpha (α)Beta (β)Gamma (γ)
Nature2 protons + 2 neutrons (He nucleus)Fast electronEM radiation (photon)
Charge+2−10
Relative mass4 (heaviest)~1/20000
Ionising abilityStrongestModerateWeakest
PenetrationStopped by paper/skinStopped by ~3 mm AlReduced by thick lead
In electric/magnetic fieldsDeflected → negative plateDeflected → positive plateNot deflected
📚 Key Vocabulary
TermCAIE DefinitionExam tip
IsotopesAtoms with the same proton number (Z) but different nucleon numbers (A)Must state BOTH conditions
Background radiationRadiation always present in the environment from natural sourcesSources: radon, rocks, food, cosmic rays
Half-lifeTime taken for half the nuclei of a particular isotope in any sample to decayAlso: time for activity/count rate to halve
Nuclear fissionA large unstable nucleus splits into two smaller nuclei, releasing energy and further neutronsChain reaction: neutrons trigger further fission
Nuclear fusionLight nuclei join to form a heavier nucleus, releasing a large amount of energyPowers all main-sequence stars
Mode
Click Check after each question, or open Walkthrough at any time.
🧠 MCQ Practice — 10 Questions — Topic 5
1Isotopes and nuclide notation
A nucleus of element X is represented as 5626X.

Which is an isotope of element X?
A2656X
B5426X
C5624X
D5428X
How to think through it
1In AZX, the bottom number Z is the proton number. The top number A is the nucleon number.
2Isotopes are the same element, so the proton number must stay the same.
3Now look for the choice where the bottom number stays 26, but the top number is different from 56.
⚠Common trap: Do not swap the top and bottom numbers. Changing the bottom number means it is a different element, not an isotope.
2Corrected count rate calculation
A radioactive source is placed near a detector connected to a counter. 210 counts are recorded by the counter in 3 minutes. The background count rate is 20 counts per minute (cpm).

What is the corrected count rate for the radioactive source?
A50 cpm
B70 cpm
C190 cpm
D270 cpm
How to think through it
1First convert the detector reading into a rate: 210 counts in 3 minutes means divide by 3 to get counts per minute.
2The detector also counts background radiation, so subtract the background rate of 20 cpm.
3Keep the units as cpm throughout the calculation.
⚠Common trap: Convert to counts per minute before subtracting the background. Mixing “counts” and “counts per minute” gives the wrong option.
3Nuclear change during beta emission
Which change is occurring in a nucleus during β-emission?
AAn electron and a neutron become one proton.
BAn electron and a proton become one neutron.
CA neutron becomes one proton and one electron.
DA proton becomes one neutron and one electron.
How to think through it
1In beta-minus emission, the change happens inside the nucleus.
2One neutron changes into one proton and an electron is emitted as the beta particle.
⚠Common trap: The beta particle is not an orbital electron from the outside of the atom. It is produced in the nuclear change and then emitted.
4Half-life from a decay graph with background
The graph shows how the count rate registered by a counter near a sample of a radioactive isotope changes over a few days. The background count rate is 5 counts per minute.
What is the half-life of the isotope?
A2.0 days
B2.5 days
C3.0 days
D4.0 days
How to think through it
1The graph reading includes the source plus the background count rate.
2At the start, subtract the 5 cpm background to find the source count rate only.
3Halve the source count rate, then add 5 cpm back to locate the matching reading on the graph.
4Read the time where the curve reaches that graph reading.
⚠Common trap: Do not halve the graph reading directly. The background count rate does not halve; only the source count rate halves.
5Shielding a mixed radioactive source
A radioactive source that emits α-particles, β-particles and γ-radiation is stored safely in a container.

From which material should the container be made?
Aaluminium
Bcopper
Cpaper
Dlead
How to think through it
1The source emits all three types of radiation: alpha, beta and gamma.
2Choose the material that deals with the most penetrating radiation.
3Alpha is stopped by paper, beta by aluminium, but gamma needs dense shielding such as lead.
⚠Common trap: Do not choose a material just because it stops alpha or beta. A safe storage container must also reduce gamma radiation.
6Correcting for background radiation
A student is investigating the count rate of a radioactive substance.

How must he adjust his reading for the background count rate?
AAdd the background count to his reading.
BIgnore the background count as it will not affect his reading.
CSubtract the background count from his reading.
DTake repeat readings to eliminate the background count.
How to think through it
1A detector reading includes two things: radiation from the source and background radiation.
2To find the count rate from the source alone, remove the background part from the reading.
3Repeating readings can make a result more reliable, but it does not make background radiation disappear.
⚠Common trap: Background correction is a subtraction step. Repeats help with random variation, not with removing the background contribution.
7Corrected count rate graph after subtracting background
The dashed line on the graph shows the decay curve recorded from a sample of a particular radioactive isotope. The count rate includes background radiation.

Which curve shows the corrected count rate for the decay?
AGraph A
BGraph B
CGraph C
DGraph D
How to think through it
1The dashed curve includes background radiation.
2Correcting means subtracting the same average background count from every point, so the whole curve moves down by a uniform amount.
3Between the close-looking choices, check whether the curve has been shifted down evenly rather than made steeper or squeezed. The shape should stay the same.
4After background is removed, the corrected count rate should approach zero, not the background count rate.
⚠Common trap: Do not halve or rescale the curve. Subtract the same background value from all readings.
8Beta absorption and paper thickness control
The diagram shows a system to control the thickness of paper produced by a machine.

The graph shows how the count rate at the detector varies with the thickness of the paper.

The rollers squeeze the paper when the count rate at the detector drops below 12 counts per second and then stop squeezing when the count rate rises above 13 counts per second.
What is the thickness of the paper coming out of the machine?
Abetween 0.40 mm and 0.50 mm
Bbetween 0.50 mm and 0.60 mm
Cbetween 0.60 mm and 0.70 mm
Dbetween 0.70 mm and 0.80 mm
How to think through it
1Thicker paper absorbs more beta particles, so the count rate at the detector becomes lower.
2The machine starts squeezing when the count rate is below 12 counts/s and stops when it rises above 13 counts/s.
3Use the graph to read the paper thickness at 12 counts/s and at 13 counts/s.
4The final thickness must lie between those two thickness readings.
⚠Common trap: Read across from the count rate to the curve, then down to the thickness axis. Do not read the y-axis values as the thickness.
9Radiation safety precautions for gamma sources
Which safety precautions must be taken when using a source of gamma radiation?

1   Reduce the distance between the source and the person.
2   Reduce the time of exposure to the radiation.
3   Use a suitable shielding material between the source and the person.

Which precautions are correct?
A1, 2 and 3
B1 and 2 only
C1 and 3 only
D2 and 3 only
How to think through it
1For radiation safety, remember the three ideas: reduce time, increase distance, and use shielding.
2Check statement 1 carefully: reducing the distance would move the person closer to the source.
3Keep only the precautions that reduce the dose received.
⚠Common trap: The distance precaution is normally to increase the distance. The wording “reduce the distance” is a deliberate trap.
10Spontaneous and random radioactive decay
Radioactive decay is a change in an unstable nucleus which may result in emission of alpha-particles or beta-particles.

Which statement describes how these emissions happen?
AAlpha emission is a spontaneous and random process but beta emission is not.
BAlpha emission and beta emission are both spontaneous and random processes.
CBeta emission is a spontaneous and random process but alpha emission is not.
DNeither alpha emission nor beta emission are spontaneous and random processes.
How to think through it
1Radioactive decay is spontaneous: it happens without any external influence.
2Radioactive decay is random: we cannot predict exactly which unstable nucleus will decay next.
3These ideas apply to both alpha emission and beta emission.
⚠Common trap: Half-life predicts the average behaviour of many nuclei. It does not let you predict the exact decay of one nucleus.
🌍 Topic 6

Space Physics

10 past-paper MCQs · Solar System and Life Cycle of Stars only

⛔ Exam scope: Solar System and Life Cycle of Stars only. 6.2.3 Universe (redshift, Hubble, Big Bang) is NOT assessed.
🌍 Solar System

Contents of the Solar System

  • Sun — medium-sized star; mostly H and He; contains most of the Solar System's mass; radiates mainly Infrared (IR), visible and Ultraviolet (UV).
  • 8 planets (in order): Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune
    Mnemonic: "My Very Educated Mother Just Served Us Noodles"
    Inner 4: rocky & small  |  Outer 4: gaseous & large (accretion model)
  • Dwarf planets: Ceres (asteroid belt), Pluto, Eris, Makemake, Haumea (Kuiper Belt)
  • Asteroids (mainly in asteroid belt between Mars & Jupiter)
  • Comets — icy bodies with elliptical orbits; Sun at a FOCUS not the centre; speed up near the Sun
  • Moons — natural satellites orbiting planets

Orbital mechanics

  • Gravity of the Sun provides the centripetal force for planetary orbits
  • v = 2πr/T for circular orbital speed; orbital speed decreases as distance increases
  • g at a planet's surface depends on the planet's mass; g decreases with distance
  • Light travel time: t = distance ÷ (3 × 10⁸ m/s)
⭐ Life Cycle of Stars

Stellar life cycle diagram

Source: Official IGCSE Physics textbook

Sun-like stars (mass < 8 solar masses)

☁️ Nebula→ Protostar→ 🌟 Main sequence→ 🔴 Red giant→ Planetary nebula→ ⚪ White dwarf
  • Protostar: interstellar cloud collapses under gravity; temperature rises
  • Main sequence: H fusion → He; outward radiation pressure balances inward gravity
  • Red giant: H fuel runs out; star expands and cools
  • White dwarf: outer layers shed as planetary nebula; hot dense core remains

Massive stars (mass > 8 solar masses)

🌟 Main sequence→ 🔴 Red supergiant→ 💥 Supernova→ ⚫ Neutron star / Black hole
  • Supernova: violent explosion; heavier elements created and dispersed into nebula
  • The nebula from a supernova may form new stars with orbiting planets
  • Neutron star or black hole depending on remaining mass
📚 Key Vocabulary
TermCAIE DefinitionExam tip
Light-yearThe distance travelled by light in one year in the vacuum of space; 1 ly = 9.5 × 10¹⁵ mIt is a DISTANCE, not a time
Nuclear fusion (in stars)The joining of light nuclei (e.g. hydrogen) to form a heavier nucleus (e.g. helium), releasing a large amount of energyPowers all main-sequence stars
Mode
Click Check after each question, or open Walkthrough at any time.
🧠 MCQ Practice — 10 Questions — Topic 6
1Orbital shape of comets
Comets move round the Sun.

Which statement describes the orbit of a comet?
AThe orbit is circular with the Sun at the centre of the circle.
BThe orbit is circular with the Sun not at the centre of the circle.
CThe orbit is elliptical with the Sun at the centre of the ellipse.
DThe orbit is elliptical with the Sun not at the centre of the ellipse.
How to think through it
1Comets do not normally travel in circular paths.
2Look for an elliptical orbit.
3In an ellipse, the Sun is at one focus, not at the centre.
⚠Watch for: “Centre” is the trap word. For a comet, think: ellipse + Sun off-centre.
2Possible result of a supernova explosion
What is a possible result of a supernova explosion?
AA neutron star
BA red giant star
CA red supergiant star
DA white dwarf star
How to think through it
1A supernova is the explosion near the end of a massive star's life.
2After the explosion, the remaining object may be extremely dense.
3Eliminate stages that happen before the explosion, and eliminate the low-mass-star ending.
⚠Watch for: Red supergiant comes before supernova. White dwarf belongs to Sun-like stars.
3Orbital period using speed = distance ÷ time
An artificial satellite orbits planet X at a distance r = 7.0 × 106 m from the centre of planet X. The average orbital speed v of the satellite is 3500 m/s.

What is the orbital period?
A21 minutes
B52 minutes
C1 hour and 45 minutes
D3 hours and 29 minutes
How to think through it
1This is just the standard speed-distance-time idea: time = distance ÷ speed.
2For one complete circular orbit, the distance travelled is the circumference: 2πr.
3Substitute r and v into T = 2πr ÷ v, then convert seconds into minutes or hours.
⚠Watch for: Do not use r as the distance travelled. The satellite travels around the whole circle.
4Orbital periods of Earth and Moon
Which statement is correct?
AThe Earth orbits the Sun once in approximately 24 hours.
BThe Earth orbits the Moon once in approximately 1 week.
CThe Moon orbits the Earth once in approximately 1 month.
DThe Sun orbits the Earth once in approximately 365 days.
How to think through it
1Separate rotation from orbit: a day is rotation; a year/month is orbital motion.
2Earth orbits the Sun in about 365 days.
3The Moon is a natural satellite of Earth, so it orbits Earth in about one month.
⚠Watch for: The Sun does not orbit the Earth in the Solar System model used here.
5Nuclear reaction powering stable stars
Stars are powered by nuclear reactions that release energy.

Which statement describes the nuclear reaction in a stable star?
ANuclear fission converts helium into hydrogen.
BNuclear fission converts hydrogen into helium.
CNuclear fusion converts helium into hydrogen.
DNuclear fusion converts hydrogen into helium.
How to think through it
1Stable stars are powered by nuclear fusion, not fission.
2In main-sequence stars, hydrogen nuclei join to form helium.
⚠Watch for: Fission means splitting nuclei. Fusion means joining light nuclei.
6Protostar transition to stable main-sequence star
What happens when a protostar becomes a stable star?
AMost of the hydrogen is converted to helium and the protostar expands.
BThe inward force due to gravity is balanced by an outward force due to the high temperature.
CThe protostar collapses due to gravity and the temperature increases.
DThe protostar runs out of hydrogen as fuel for the nuclear reaction.
How to think through it
1A protostar becomes stable when fusion begins and the forces balance.
2Gravity pulls inward. High temperature and radiation pressure produce an outward effect.
3Look for the option describing a balance, not continued collapse or running out of fuel.
⚠Watch for: “Runs out of hydrogen” happens later, after a long main-sequence stage.
7Orbital duration of a newly discovered planet
The table gives the orbital durations of some planets in the Solar System.

A newly discovered planet has an orbital radius approximately 1.7 times the orbital radius of Earth.

What is the best estimate of the orbital duration of this planet?
PlanetMercuryVenusEarthJupiterSaturnUranusNeptune
Orbital duration / days88225365433010 70030 60059 800
A295 days
B690 days
C7100 days
D20 600 days
How to think through it
1Start with Earth: its orbital duration is 365 days.
2A larger orbital radius means a longer orbital duration.
3The planet is only 1.7 times Earth's orbital radius, so it should be longer than Earth but far shorter than Jupiter.
4Pick the option that is roughly around two Earth years, not thousands of days.
⚠Watch for: This is an estimate from the table trend. Do not jump to the outer planets.
8Star life cycle — identifying the correct statement
Which statement about the life cycle of a star is correct?
AAll stars eventually run out of hydrogen as fuel.
BA red giant forms a planetary nebula with a supernova at its centre.
CA white dwarf star eventually forms a black hole.
DMost stars expand and form protostars.
How to think through it
1Check whether each statement fits both Sun-like stars and massive stars.
2All main-sequence stars use hydrogen as fuel, and this fuel eventually runs out.
3Low-mass and high-mass stars have different endings, so reject statements that mix the two pathways.
⚠Watch for: Planetary nebula and white dwarf belong to Sun-like stars. Supernova, neutron star and black hole belong to massive stars.
9Life cycle order for a massive star
In which order are the objects listed formed during the life cycle of a massive star?
Aprotostar → red giant → supernova → stable star
Bprotostar → stable star → red giant → supernova
Cred giant → stable star → protostar → supernova
Dred giant → supernova → protostar → stable star
How to think through it
1Every star begins as a protostar before becoming a stable star.
2A massive star then expands into a red supergiant before exploding as a supernova.
3Find the option that keeps this order. Anything starting with red giant is already far into a star's life cycle.
⚠Watch for: In IGCSE MCQs, “red giant” may sometimes be used loosely in the option, but the order is still the key idea.
10Converting metres to light-years
The galaxy Draco II is a distance of 6.6 × 10²⁰ m from Earth.

What is this distance in light-years?
A1.4 × 10⁴ light-years
B6.9 × 10⁴ light-years
C1.4 × 10⁶ light-years
D6.9 × 10⁶ light-years
How to think through it
1A light-year is a distance, not a time.
2One light-year ≈ 9.5 × 10¹⁵ m.
3Divide the distance in metres by 9.5 × 10¹⁵ to convert it into light-years.
4Use powers of 10 carefully: 10²⁰ ÷ 10¹⁵ gives about 10⁵, then the numbers reduce it slightly.
⚠Watch for: The exponent is the main trap in this question.
